sentences.text: Sure.
Let's see if we can go to it.
If not, I have coasters in here, too.
Laura Hillman.
And it's L-A-U-R-A H-I-L-L-M-A-N.
Hannelore Wolff.
H-A-N-N-E-L-O-R-E W-O-L-F-F.
I was born October 16, 1923, in Aurich, Germany.
It was Ostfriesland, East Friesland, near the North Sea.
Martin and Karoline Wolff.
Also Wolff.
There were a lot of Wolffs in our little town.
Distantly.
Rosel, Hildagard, then myself, and my brothers Wolfgang, and Selly.
Proportionately, there were a lot of Jewish people in our little town.
I believe we were 200 families.
And life centered around the synagogue and Jewish life.
We would walk to synagogue with my father on Friday nights.
And Saturday mornings, also with my mother.
And we had lots of youth clubs, a good [INAUDIBLE], and others.
And Saturday afternoon was a time to get together with family and friends.
It was comfortable.
It was spiritual as well because my mother would read to us from a book called [NON-ENGLISH].
And I remember it so well because it always touched on something I had to know each and every week, year after year.
Both my grandfathers died when I was very young.
I do remember that.
But vaguely.
But the grandmothers were the matriarchs of the family.
We would pay our respects and go and get a Shabbos blessing.
And we had to curtsy.
And she would look us over and see if we walked straight enough or if our shoes were polished well.
But it was loving, very loving.
Oh, holidays were wonderful.
The entire week before, the house was readied and all the foods.
Many people were invited.
And my father would always bring home stray people from the synagogue.
Not only on holidays, but Friday nights, too.
They'd eat with us, and he'd send them off with some money or shirts that they needed or jackets.
It was a giving atmosphere.
School was a small Jewish school.
In retrospect, I can see that we learned so much in this little school.
Our teacher exposed us not only to secular studies, but in the afternoon, we had Hebrew and Yiddish, and all that went with it.
And when it came time for me to go to high school, I couldn't go already.
Only my oldest sister could go to the lyceum.
So my parents later sent me to a school in Berlin.
I made my abitur privately.
And I went to Dr.
Frenkel's kindergarten seminar in Berlin, in Grunewald.
Very much.
They lived on our block.
They came to us for Passover and Hanukkah.
We would go to their house for Easter and Weihnachten.
But we were told we couldn't eat the meal, just the sweets we could have.
And we played together until Hitler came.
And then it all changed.
Well, I remember one incident when my brother's friend stood across the street and said to him, we can't play with you anymore.
And Wolfgang said, why not, you're my best friend.
And he said, no, my father is in the SR now.
And he said, you're Jews.
And we're going to take your house away soon because we like your house better than ours.
And from then on, they would throw stones at us and ostracize us.
And it was hazardous to even walk to school or take our bicycles to school.
We were often assaulted by people who had been our friends before.
Oh, I must have been 12, 13.
The incident with these children on our block.
That was the first.
And then of course the next major thing was that we had to leave our homes.
And it wasn't a real ghetto with gates, but we had to move into an area of the old city, narrow streets, and small places to live with others.
And from there, I remember Kristallnacht from that place.
It had to be before Kristallnacht because I remember Kristallnacht walking out of that house.
There was an edict that we all had to leave our homes.
And of course, most of our possessions were left behind, I believe.
At that time, my parents were able to sell some of it.
But how could one sell anything profitable in such a short time?
So it was a sale under duress.
It had to have been before '38, as I said before.
And then the Kristallnacht remains very much in my memory, you know, as if it had been yesterday.
We heard glass breaking.
And I didn't know where it came from.
And loud noises and boots marching.
And I went into my parents' bedroom.
It was late at night.
And I said Mama, what is this?
What's going on?
And both my parents, they woke my younger brothers and said to us, we don't know exactly what it is.
But it can't be good.
Put on a lot of clothes.
It's very cold.
And stay close together.
Stay with us.
And then my father tried to bless us, like he did on Friday nights.
But he didn't even finish because our door was broken into.
And they stormed-- these brown shirts with black boots and armbands-- came in and said, Jews, raus!
And they lined us all up.
And across the street from our house was a department store, small department store owned by Jews.
And all the windows were broken.
And the people carried out the material and whatever there was.
And we were marched through the town to end up--
Because the door was knocked in.
And the storm troopers were in our house, saying, raus Juden, raus.
And I could see across the street the department store was being looted.
All the big glass windows had been broken.
And I saw friends and relatives and neighbors all lining up.
And they marched us through the streets until we came in front of the synagogue.
The synagogue was engulfed in flames.
I could see fragments of prayer books and Torahs just burning.
And my little brothers were terrified, of course.
And as we stood there and watched, they kicked us with their boots and their rifles and insulted us with curse words I'd never heard before.
And then finally in the early morning, they took all the men and marched them to one side.
And women and children could go home.
I remember going home with my little brothers and my mother, my grandmother.
And we prayed all day.
We prayed.
We had the siddur open, and we prayed.
And we waited.
We had no idea what had happened to our men.
Late that afternoon, towards evening, my father walked in.
And he told us that all the men were being carted off to a concentration camp.
Didn't know which one.
He had been let go, and he didn't know really why.
But he limped.
He had a severe injury from the First World War.
He had the Iron Cross for bravery and all that.
He said it could be that one of the Nazis spoke up on his behalf.
We never know for sure.
My father was one of the very few men who came home.
The others all went to Buchenwald.
A week later, we heard that my uncle had been killed.
That was the first casualty.
And so from then on things progressed.
And we couldn't work where we wanted to.
We couldn't go to school.
We were not allowed to own radios or anything of value.
I went away to school.
My brothers went to Cologne to live in an orphanage and go to school there.
And my parents had to leave Aurich.
All Jews had to leave the entire region of Ostfriesland.
Ostfriesland was made judenrein, free of Jews.
There were very few choices were people could go.
Berlin was one.
And Weimar and [INAUDIBLE] was another.
Since one of my grandmothers was in Weimar living with a daughter, my mother elected to go there to be able to help her mother.
They lived in very cramped quarters.
My parents lived under the roof in her house.
It was owned by two sisters.
My father was doing forced labor there.
And one day I got a letter telling me that my mother had gotten an urn from Buchenwald and a letter that my father had died.
She had not told me that my father had been arrested six weeks earlier, just being taken off the street for riding a bicycle without a permit.
I didn't know one needed a permit.
And he didn't either.
It was a ruse just to arrest him.
And I had wondered why I hadn't gotten a letter.
And I was very uneasy about that.
But then came this shocking news.
And my world collapsed, of course.
My father, who had sang songs to me.
And you know, the fantasies I had of what happened to him, how did he die.
And I was still in school in Berlin.

And a month later, or perhaps a little less, my mother wrote another letter saying she had gotten a deportation notice saying that she was being relocated to the east, that my brothers had gotten the same notice in Cologne.
And they were to leave on May 8, 1942 to somewhere in the east.
I remember that day very well at school.
It was raining very hard.
And after I read the letter, I stormed out of the room.
And I had to sort out my feelings.
What was I going to do?
I came back to the room.
I sat down and wrote a letter to the Gestapo saying, I'm Karoline Wolff's daughter, Hannelore, and I wish to go with her to be relocated in the east.
My roommates thought I was out of my mind and said, why can't you wait until tomorrow?
You'll think about it differently.
To me, there was only one way.
So I sent the letter off.
And a few weeks later, I got an official letter from the Gestapo saying that they allowed me to travel from Berlin to Weimar to be transported.
One needed a document to be on the train.
So I immediately set to work and packed a few things, mainly my books, my beloved books.
And I rode the train, on the train to Weimar to be with my mother.
And on May 8, 1942, my mother's birthday, we walked to the [NON-ENGLISH], which is an animal hall.
This is where they assembled us.
Why they picked an animal hall is anybody's guess.
It was very humiliating.
And from there, we were transported to Lublin, to the ghetto in Lublin.
We were still together.
Very cramped quarters, but that was all fine.
My mother and my two brothers.
One of my sister was out, had gone in 1939 to England.
She became a registered nurse there.
My other sister, Hildagard, who then shortened her name to Hilda, went on the one of the little boats from Yugoslavia, to then Palestine.
And they lived, of course, yes.
And so it was the four of us-- my mother, Wolfgang, Selly, and myself.
We all worked.
And one night, the Jewish police, the kapos, came to our door and said they were looking for Wolfgang Wolff.
And my mother knew what that meant.
And she said, but he's working.
He didn't get a deportation notice.
Oh, he said, we'll bring him back in the morning.
Of course, we knew that it wasn't true.
After the war, we learned that my brother had died in Majdanek, which wasn't very far from Lublin.
So it was the three of us-- my mother, Selly and I remained.
We lived with another family in one room.
There was a communal kitchen.
But there was also a little stove in the room.
We had brought with us some lentils and rice.
And we augmented that with turnips and beets and onions, whatever we could get.
We didn't have very much money left.
And the money we were paid for work were ghetto marks.
And you couldn't do very much with that.
But we managed.
We were not yet so hungry.
And then I had a friend in the Jewish police, a young man, also a German Jew, who said to me, there's going to be a liquidation of the ghetto.
But I can hide you.
I said, you can't just hide me.
You have to hide my mother and brother.
And so he said, yes, I will.
But not in the same cellar.
You'll have to be separated.
I went home, told my mother about it.
At the time, I worked for a Nazi.
I was a kindergärtnerin.
I was a nursery school teacher to his two children.
Yes.
I wrote it in my book, in my manuscript.
It'll come to me.
All right?
If not, we'll look it up in my manuscript.
And so we hid.
I was in a tunnel.
It was like an obstacle course, getting to that tunnel.
It was described to me how to get there.
This young policeman told me how I would get there.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
He told me how to get there.
Later found out that he also had me shadowed to see that no one was following, because there were a lot of informants also.
So my mother went in one cellar, and my brother in another.
And I was in this tunnel.
We had to be very quiet, of course.
And after a day or two, all the rumblings above us stopped.
And we knew the wait was over.
I came out of my tunnel and went to the little streets in the ghetto, looking for my mother and my brother.
But I didn't find them.
I never saw my mother again.
And an hour or so later, [NON-ENGLISH], the policeman, came for me.
And I accused him of not having secured my mother and brother.
And he said, I told you, there is a risk.
We could've been discovered, too.
There's nothing I could do.
I did the best.
But you have to come with me because the trucks are ready.
We are being sent to a camp called Belzyce.
It's a labor camp.
Come with me or they'll shoot you.
So we went on the truck.
And we came to Belzyce.
It was a very small labor camp.
And I met a German-Jewish doctor and his wife and daughter there.
Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And he had a small hospital-- not a hospital, really, six beds and a barrack.
And he said, you can be my nurse.
And I told him I knew nothing about nursing.
He said, it's fine.
You'll be sheltered there and you'll have a little more to eat.
And there was a stove that I could boil a potato on when I could find one.
And one day, while we were preparing a bed for a young boy who was to be brought in.
He had been severely beaten.
We were getting everything ready, the other attendant and I.
The stretcher came in, and I looked at this young boy.
And it was my brother, my brother Selly.
He was beaten up.
And he had a collapsed lung.
This young boy, this beautiful young boy, beaten to a pulp.
And I sat with him.
I held his hand.
And slowly he mended.
But the collapsed lung never healed.
And he had grown too fast for his age.
He was so young.
And nothing to eat.
And he later told me that there were babies in his cellar who started to cry.
And they couldn't suppress the cries.
And that's how he was found out.
And he said he didn't see or hear of my mother either.
He didn't know.
He could've been 14 and 1/2 or so.
This was in the late 1942.
After that, one day, again, there were rumors that Belzyce was being dissolved and we were being sent to another camp, a harsher camp called Budzyn.
And before the liquidation actually happened, some Nazis and people from the Judenrat came from a town called Krausnick.
Very small town.
And they were conferring all day long.
I could see all this from my little hospital room, [NON-ENGLISH] as we called it.
And I think they were looking for specialized labor, people who could build, people who could make boots.
And they were going to take these people with them.
Well, towards the afternoon, the Nazi party with the Judenrat stomped across the grounds and came into our hospital.
They were so drunk by then, especially the untersturmführer, who was the kommandant of this little camp in Krausnick.
He was totally drunken and he pointed to me.
I was hiding behind the stove because the sight of the uniforms intimidated me, as they would anyone else.
And he pointed to me and he said, who is that beautiful bird?
She can come with us.
We need a nurse.
And I said, I'm not a nurse, Herr Untersturmführer.
And he said, who asked you?
Look, she's afraid of me, too.
She comes with us.
And then he walked out.
And that man from the Judenrat said to me, you have no choice.
You have to come with us.
And I said I had a brother there.
And he needed me and he was ill.
And I couldn't come.
He said, oh no.
You have to come, or else I'll pay for it dearly.
So I went that afternoon.
I never saw that untersturmführer again until one fatal night.
And I'll explain that later.
He was too drunk to even remember me.
I worked in this little camp.
And it was a very unique camp.
Everybody paid to stay there.
They had their houses here.
It was unlike any camp I've ever seen.
There were people with money-- Polish Jews-- who paid everyone off, not only the untersturmführer, everyone in that little town they paid off.
And they didn't quite know what to do with me.
A German Jew, no money.
And how I'd gotten there.
And they put me to work in the office, the outer office, and told me that I was not to even notice what was going on.
Just pretend.
And all day long, German officers would come in.
And they wanted a meeting with the Judenrat man.
His name was [INAUDIBLE].
And I announced it.
I answered the telephone.
And one day, an officer came in.
His name was Schlesinger.
Schlesinger is a Jewish name.
He looked very, very Jewish.
He looked just like a man I had known in Berlin in school.
Just like that.
And my fantasy started to spin.
And I thought perhaps he's here in disguise.
And he stopped by my desk very often.
And it turned out that he had sealed the apartment of one of my mother's cousin in Hamburg.
He knew him very well, and he had bought hats and ties from this man.
The man had a haberdashery.
And he became very friendly with me.
And one day he came and he said, you know, I can help you get out of here.
You're not going to stay here very long.
There are worst things to camp to happen.
I'll help you.
Well, I believed him.
I really believed him.
And he said, why don't you get a permit from the camp people to say that you have to help me.
That you have to do some book work with me.
I did.
Nobody told me not to go.
I went out with this man.
And no sooner did he have me outside, he took me up to a room and brutalized and raped me and wanted to know what my relationship with that untersturmführer was.
He wanted to get this untersturmführer out of this position and have that for himself.
Then I came out black and blue, raped, with swollen eyes.
He beat me.
He yanked my hair.
And he was called away by a telephone call.
And that otherwise, I might never have gotten out of there.
We came back to the camp, and he shoved me inside and said, if you ever breathe a word of this, you'll be dead.
The Judenrat man, who was the head of this little camp, looked at me and he said, well, you deserved what you got.
I said, what do you mean?
And he said to me, he's the worst Jew hater there is.
He's trying to uproot us all, get us all killed here.
And he used you, and what did you tell him?
I said, why didn't you tell me?
And he said, how could I?
I don't know you.
You might have come back to him and told him.
And that same night, I was called at midnight.
And that's when I saw that same untersturmführer again, raging and ranting.
And he said there's a Jewish cemetery in town.
Just take her there, shoot her.
I don't ever want to hear or see of her again.
